findings.description: Zip Slip vulnerability: ZipEntry.getName() flows to file write without path containment check, allowing arbitrary file write outside extraction directory
findings.code_snippet:
extractFile( srcF,
                         dir, zis,
                         ze.getName(),
                         new Date( ze.getTime() ),
                         ze.isDirectory() );
findings.reasoning: The zip entry name `ze.getName()` is passed directly to `extractFile()` with no validation. Inside `extractFile()`, this name is passed to `FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName)` which resolves the path relative to the extraction directory. However, `resolveFile` does NOT contain any check that the resulting path is within `dir`. For relative paths containing `../` sequences, it simply computes `new File(baseFile, filenm).getAbsoluteFile()` and returns the result (after canonicalization). No containment check is ever performed. An attacker can craft a zip entry with name `../../etc/cron.d/evil` to write arbitrary content outside the extraction directory.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Source: ze.getName() at Expand.java:116 -> entryName parameter at Expand.java:148 -> FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName) at Expand.java:153 -> File f at Expand.java:153 -> FileOutputStream(f) at Expand.java:178
evidence.notes: The startsWith check in resolveFile (line 1381) only handles absolute paths like '/etc/passwd'. Relative traversal sequences like '../../etc/passwd' bypass this check entirely and are resolved by new File(baseFile, filenm) which correctly resolves the .. sequences to escape the base directory. No containment check is performed after resolution.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a zip file with an entry named '../../etc/cron.d/evil' that, when extracted using Expand.expandFile(), writes to /etc/cron.d/evil instead of the intended extraction directory, enabling arbitrary file write and potentially remote code execution
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to supply a zip file for extraction by the application using plexus-utils Expand class
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary file write / Remote code execution via Zip Slip

flags.message: Classic Zip Slip vulnerability (CVE pattern) in a utility library used by many downstream projects
flags.evidence: ze.getName() flows directly to FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName) with no containment check, then to FileOutputStream. The resolveFile implementation only handles absolute paths, not relative traversal sequences like ../../etc/passwd

sanity_check.reasoning: This is a textbook Zip Slip vulnerability (well-documented CWE-022 pattern). The code at Expand.java:114-118 directly passes ze.getName() to extractFile(), which at line 152-153 uses FileUtils.resolveFile() without any containment check to verify the resolved path stays within the target directory. The evidence clearly shows the dataflow from attacker-controlled zip entry name to FileOutputStream write. Being a utility library (plexus-utils) widely used in Maven ecosystem, this has high real-world exploitability whenever callers use the Expand functionality with untrusted zip files.

findings.description: Zip Slip sink in FileUtils.resolveFile: ZipEntry name flows to File construction at line 1424 with no containment check verifying the resolved path remains within the base directory
findings.reasoning: This is the sink of the Zip Slip data flow. `filenm` (derived from `ZipEntry.getName()`) is used directly to construct a `File` relative to `baseFile` (the extraction directory). The `new File(baseFile, filenm)` call resolves any `../` sequences in `filenm` relative to `baseFile`, which can traverse outside the base directory. While `getCanonicalFile()` is called at line 1428, this only resolves symlinks and normalizes the path - it does NOT check that the resulting path is still within `baseFile`. There is no `startsWith`/`Path.startsWith` containment check performed AFTER canonicalization. The resolved (potentially traversed) file is returned and written to by `Expand.extractFile()` via `FileOutputStream`.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: ZipEntry.getName() [Expand.java:116] -> entryName [Expand.java:148] -> FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, entryName) [Expand.java:153] -> filename param [FileUtils.java:1367] -> filenm [FileUtils.java:1372/1377] -> new File(baseFile, filenm) [FileUtils.java:1424] -> returned File -> FileOutputStream(f) [Expand.java:178]
evidence.notes: The containment check (startsWith at line 1381) only guards the absolute path branch. The relative path branch at line 1424 has ZERO containment validation. An entry name like '../../malicious' resolves to a path outside baseFile with no exception thrown.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker provides a zip file with entry '../../etc/passwd' which resolves to /etc/passwd when extracted to /tmp/safe-dir, enabling overwrite of arbitrary files on the filesystem
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must supply a zip file that gets processed by Expand.expandFile() or any code calling FileUtils.resolveFile() with untrusted zip entry names

flags.message: Classic Zip Slip vulnerability pattern in utility library method used for archive extraction
flags.evidence: The resolveFile method is called from Expand.java with ZipEntry.getName() and the returned File is used with FileOutputStream, forming a complete Zip Slip attack chain

sanity_check.reasoning: The code clearly shows a Zip Slip vulnerability: ZipEntry names containing path traversal sequences (e.g., '../../malicious') flow into new File(baseFile, filenm) without any subsequent check that the resolved canonical path remains within baseFile. The getCanonicalFile() call normalizes the path but does not perform any containment validation. This is a well-known vulnerability pattern in archive extraction code, and the evidence shows a complete data flow from ZipEntry.getName() to FileOutputStream, making it genuinely exploitable in any application using this library to extract zip archives.

findings.description: Zip Slip sink in FileUtils.resolveFile absolute path branch: ZipEntry name containing an absolute path flows to File construction at line 1383 with no containment check, allowing arbitrary file write to any absolute path on the filesystem
findings.reasoning: This is the absolute path branch of `resolveFile`. When a ZipEntry name starts with `/` (the file separator on Unix/Linux), `resolveFile` detects it as an absolute path, constructs `new File(filenm)` directly from the absolute path string, and returns it with no containment check whatsoever. This allows a zip entry named `/etc/cron.d/evil` or `/root/.ssh/authorized_keys` to be written to arbitrary absolute filesystem locations. The `getCanonicalFile()` call merely normalizes the path but performs no boundary enforcement. This is the absolute path variant of Zip Slip (CWE-036).

evidence.dataflow_analysis: ZipEntry.getName() returns '/etc/passwd' [Expand.java:116] -> entryName='/etc/passwd' [Expand.java:148] -> FileUtils.resolveFile(dir, '/etc/passwd') [Expand.java:153] -> filenm='/etc/passwd' [FileUtils.java:1367] -> startsWith('/') = true [FileUtils.java:1381] -> new File('/etc/passwd') [FileUtils.java:1383] -> returned -> FileOutputStream('/etc/passwd') [Expand.java:178]
evidence.notes: The ZIP format does not restrict entry names to relative paths. An attacker can craft a ZIP with entries like '/etc/passwd', '/root/.ssh/authorized_keys', or 'C:\Windows\System32\evil.dll' (on Windows with colon detection). These bypass even the relative-path traversal concern and go directly to arbitrary absolute paths.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a zip with an entry named '/etc/cron.d/backdoor' (absolute path); when extracted via Expand.expandFile(), FileUtils.resolveFile returns the absolute File object and Expand.extractFile writes to /etc/cron.d/backdoor regardless of the intended extraction directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must supply a zip file processed by Expand.expandFile(); the zip entry name must start with the path separator character (/ on Linux)
exploit_payload.category: Absolute path traversal / Arbitrary file write

flags.message: Exploitability depends on callers passing ZipEntry names directly to resolveFile without sanitization
flags.evidence: The vulnerability is real in the context of Expand.java usage, but as a library function, resolveFile itself is a general-purpose utility. The evidence shows Expand.java does pass ze.getName() directly, making the exploit chain complete.

flags.message: This is a well-known Zip Slip pattern but applied to the absolute path variant specifically
flags.evidence: Most Zip Slip analyses focus on relative path traversal (../../etc/passwd), but this finding specifically identifies the absolute path branch (/etc/passwd) which bypasses even relative-path guards that some implementations add.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code snippet confirms the vulnerability: when `filenm` starts with File.separator, a new File object is created directly from the absolute path string with no containment check against a base directory, and `getCanonicalFile()` only normalizes rather than validates. The dataflow evidence from Expand.java shows that ZipEntry names are passed directly to this function, completing an exploitable Zip Slip chain that allows arbitrary absolute path writes. This is a legitimate critical vulnerability in a library that processes zip files.

findings.description: FileUtils.catPath only handles `../` at the beginning of the `path` argument; embedded traversal sequences (e.g., `subdir/../../etc/passwd`) are silently appended without normalization, allowing path traversal attacks in any caller that trusts the return value as a safe path.
findings.reasoning: The `catPath` method claims to properly handle `..` sequences when concatenating paths (per Javadoc). However, the `while` loop only strips leading `../` sequences (those at the very start of the `path` argument). If the user-supplied `path` contains traversal sequences that are NOT at the beginning — e.g., `"img/../../../etc/passwd"` — the loop never executes and the raw traversal path is returned verbatim as `"/base/dir/img/../../../etc/passwd"`. This output is not canonicalized, so any downstream file operation (open, read, write) using this return value will resolve the `..` segments and escape the intended base directory. The method is public API documented as safe, making it particularly dangerous for callers who rely on it for path safety.

evidence.code_context: The while loop condition `pth.startsWith("../")` only processes traversal at the start of `path`. Embedded sequences like `img/../../../etc/passwd` bypass the loop entirely and are appended raw: `new StringBuffer(lookup).append("/").append(pth).toString()` returns `/var/www/html/img/../../../etc/passwd`. No canonicalization is applied to the result.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: User input -> path parameter -> pth variable (no sanitization if not starting with ../) -> final string concatenation -> returned to caller -> used in file operations resolving traversal sequences
evidence.notes: The method is only called from tests in this codebase, but it is a public static API in a widely used library (plexus-utils). Any downstream caller relying on catPath for path safety is vulnerable. The Javadoc implies traversal is handled correctly.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies a path argument containing an embedded `../` sequence (not at the start) to `catPath`, which returns a traversal path. Any caller using this return value in a file operation reads/writes outside the intended base directory.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must control the `path` argument passed to catPath, or control input flowing through a caller of this API.

flags.message: Incomplete path traversal handling in public API method
flags.evidence: The while loop only handles leading ../ sequences; embedded traversal like img/../../../etc/passwd bypasses sanitization entirely and is returned verbatim

flags.message: Exploitability depends on callers trusting the return value as a safe path
flags.evidence: As a library utility method with misleading Javadoc implying full traversal handling, downstream callers relying on this for security are vulnerable; the code is production library code, not test code

sanity_check.reasoning: The vulnerability is accurately described and confirmed by code inspection: the while loop condition `pth.startsWith('../')` only strips leading traversal sequences, leaving embedded sequences like `img/../../../etc/passwd` completely unhandled and appended raw to the result. This is a public static API in a widely-used library (plexus-utils) with Javadoc that implies traversal is handled correctly, making it a legitimate security vulnerability for callers that trust the return value as safe. The finding is a true positive representing incomplete path traversal protection in a production utility library.

findings.description: False positive: getRelativePath startsWith check is not a security containment check
findings.reasoning: This `startsWith` call in `getRelativePath(String basedir, String filename)` is used to check whether the filename is relative to the basedir for the purpose of computing a relative path string. It is NOT a security containment check — the method never opens, reads, or writes any files. It only returns a computed relative path string. No filesystem I/O occurs in `PathTool` at all. The startsWith check here is a functional pre-condition to path computation logic, not an access control boundary. There is no exploitable path traversal vulnerability.

evidence.notes: PathTool is a pure string utility for computing relative path strings. No filesystem access occurs anywhere in the class. The startsWith calls are functional checks, not security access controls.

flags.message: startsWith check is a functional path computation guard, not a security access control
flags.evidence: The method getRelativePath only computes and returns a relative path string. No filesystem I/O is performed anywhere in PathTool. The startsWith check is a pre-condition to avoid computing a path when filename is not under basedir.

flags.message: No filesystem access occurs in this class, making path traversal exploitation impossible
flags.evidence: PathTool is a pure string utility class. The code returns an empty string or a computed relative path string — it never opens, reads, writes, or accesses any file. CWE-022 (path traversal) requires actual filesystem access to be exploitable.

flags.message: CWE-022 is inapplicable to a pure string manipulation utility with no filesystem operations
flags.evidence: The file is categorized as a utility/file-system layer library, but PathTool performs no actual filesystem operations. Applying CWE-022 here is a category error.

sanity_check.reasoning: This finding is a false positive. The code in question is a pure string manipulation utility (PathTool) that computes relative path strings without performing any filesystem I/O. The startsWith check at line 73 is a functional guard to ensure the filename is under the basedir before computing a relative path — it is not a security access control boundary. CWE-022 (Path Traversal) requires actual filesystem access to be exploitable, which is entirely absent here.

findings.description: False positive: getRelativePath single-arg startsWith check is not a security containment check
findings.reasoning: This `startsWith` call in the single-argument `getRelativePath(String filename)` checks if the filename begins with a path separator character (/ or \). If not, it prepends one to normalize the filename before computing the relative path. This is pure path normalization, not a security access control. No file I/O occurs; the method only returns a computed string. Not exploitable as a path traversal vulnerability.

evidence.notes: Pure normalization - adds leading separator if missing. No security boundary enforced.

flags.message: The startsWith check is pure path normalization, not a security boundary
flags.evidence: The code adds a leading separator if missing, then delegates to determineRelativePath. No file I/O occurs, no access control is enforced, and no security-sensitive operation is performed.

flags.message: No exploitable path traversal exists here
flags.evidence: The method returns a computed string only. There is no file system access, no permission check being bypassed, and the normalization logic is clearly documented in the surrounding comment block.

sanity_check.reasoning: The flagged code is straightforward path normalization that prepends a separator character if the filename lacks one. This is a utility method that computes a relative path string without performing any file I/O or security enforcement, making CWE-022 inapplicable. The original verdict of false positive with high confidence is correct and should be rejected.

findings.description: False positive: calculateLink startsWith/contains checks are URL/path routing logic, not security containment
findings.reasoning: All `startsWith` and `contains` calls in `calculateLink()` (lines 205, 211, 217, 225, 234, 239) are used for URL/path joining and routing logic — e.g., detecting historical `/site/` prefix, detecting absolute links `/absolute/`, trapping URLs with `:` (like `http://`), and combining relative path components correctly. None of these are security access control mechanisms. The method returns a computed path/URL string and performs no file I/O. These are false positives generated by a custom query that flags all `startsWith` uses in PathTool methods.

evidence.code_context: calculateLink(String link, String relativePath) - pure string utility for combining link and relative path for web site generation. No filesystem access. startsWith used for routing/joining logic only.
evidence.notes: Lines 205, 211, 217, 225, 234, 239 all false positives - URL/link construction logic, no security boundary.

flags.message: Path traversal detection flagged pure string utility function
flags.evidence: calculateLink() is a pure string manipulation utility for web site link generation with no filesystem I/O. The startsWith/contains checks are routing/joining logic, not security boundaries.

flags.message: No filesystem access means no path traversal risk
flags.evidence: The method only concatenates strings to form relative URLs for static site generation. It never opens files, accesses directories, or performs any I/O that could be exploited via path traversal.

flags.message: CWE-022 requires actual filesystem or resource access to be applicable
flags.evidence: CWE-022 (Path Traversal) is about improper neutralization of special path elements used in OS file system operations. This code returns a string; the caller is responsible for safe use of the returned value.

sanity_check.reasoning: The `calculateLink()` method in PathTool.java is a pure string utility that combines link and relative path strings for web site generation. It performs no filesystem access, no I/O, and no security enforcement — the `startsWith` and `contains` checks are purely for URL/path routing and joining logic. Flagging this as CWE-022 path traversal is a false positive, as the method has no mechanism to actually traverse a filesystem and any security concern would be the caller's responsibility.

findings.description: False positive: getRelativeFilePath startsWith checks are Windows drive letter normalization, not security containment
findings.reasoning: All `startsWith` calls in `getRelativeFilePath()` (lines 324, 328, 335, 343, 344) are checking for Windows drive letters (colon at position 1, e.g., `C:`). These checks are used to: (1) lowercase Windows drive letters for normalization, and (2) detect when two paths are on different Windows drives (in which case no relative path exists). None of these are security access control mechanisms — they are pure path normalization for cross-platform compatibility. The method computes and returns a relative path string only; it performs no file I/O. These are false positives.

evidence.notes: Lines 324, 328, 335, 343, 344 all check for Windows drive letters at position 1. Pure path normalization/computation logic. No file I/O. No security boundary enforced.

flags.message: No security vulnerability present - code is Windows drive letter normalization logic
flags.evidence: All startsWith(':', 1) checks are detecting Windows drive letters (e.g., 'C:') for path normalization and cross-drive detection. No file I/O occurs, no security boundary is enforced, and the method purely computes relative paths.

flags.message: CWE-022 (Path Traversal) incorrectly applied to path computation utility
flags.evidence: PathTool.getRelativeFilePath() is a pure computation method that calculates a relative path string. It does not perform any file system access, does not open files, and cannot be exploited for path traversal. The 'vulnerability' flagged is Windows drive letter normalization code (lowercasing drive letters, detecting cross-drive scenarios).

sanity_check.reasoning: This is a confirmed false positive. The code in PathTool.java performs Windows drive letter normalization and cross-drive detection as part of computing a relative file path string. There is no file I/O, no security boundary, and no path traversal vulnerability. The startsWith(':', 1) checks are standard cross-platform path handling logic. CWE-022 is completely inapplicable here.
